cards.side1: Why is a polystyrene cup used for calorimetry experiments instead of a glass beaker?
cards.side2: Polystyrene is a better thermal insulator than glass, so it reduces heat loss to the surroundings, giving a more accurate temperature change reading.

cards.side1: How can you determine an accurate maximum temperature change if the reaction is slow or loses heat?
cards.side2: Plot a graph of temperature against time. Draw a line of best fit for the temperatures before addition and a line of best fit for the temperatures during cooling. Extrapolate the cooling curve back to the time of mixing to find the theoretical max temp.

cards.side1: What is the formula to calculate the heat energy transferred (Q) in a reaction mixture?
cards.side2: $Q = mc\Delta T$, where m is the mass of the solution (assuming $1\text{ cm}^3 = 1\text{ g}$), c is the specific heat capacity ($4.18\text{ J g}^{-1}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ for water), and ΔT is the temperature change.

cards.side1: How do you calculate the molar enthalpy change (ΔH) from the heat energy (Q)?
cards.side2: $\Delta H = -\frac{Q}{n}$, where n is the number of moles reacting. Make sure to convert Q from Joules to kilojoules. Add a '-' sign for exothermic, '+' sign for endothermic.

cards.side1: What are the common safety hazards when performing a neutralizing enthalpy change experiment?
cards.side2: Concentrated acids and alkalis are corrosive. Wear eye protection. Handle thermometers carefully to prevent breakage.

cards.side1: What is the most significant source of error in simple calorimetry experiments?
cards.side2: Heat loss to the surroundings (or heat absorbed from the surroundings in endothermic reactions).

cards.side1: How can the apparatus be modified to improve thermal insulation?
cards.side2: Place a lid on the polystyrene cup and place the cup inside a glass beaker packed with cotton wool for added insulation.

cards.side1: What assumption made in the calculations is a potential source of minor error?
cards.side2: Assuming the specific heat capacity and density of the reaction mixture are exactly the same as pure water ($4.18\text{ J g}^{-1}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ and $1\text{ g cm}^{-3}$).
